channel.title: 4. Strengthen The Argument Questions on Arnesh's Website
channel.description: Recent content in 4. Strengthen The Argument Questions on Arnesh's Website

item.title: 4.1 Introduction
item.description: Lesson Content Strengthen the Argument questions are among the most common types of GMAT Critical Reasoning Questions. The good news is that, as promised, the skills that you learned in the Assumption Chapter – and reinforced in the Weaken the Argument Chapter – will be significant assets to you as you learn to handle Strengthen the Argument questions. Let’s begin discussing how to effectively strengthen an argument.

item.title: 4.2 Understanding How to Effectively Strengthen an Argument
item.description: Lesson Content As just mentioned, strengthening an argument involves providing new information that strengthens the support for the argument’s conclusion. To see strengthening in action, let’s consider an example: Alice knows all about making smoothies, and she makes smoothies that all her friends like. Clearly, her new smoothie shop will be a success. We can strengthen that argument by providing new information that makes the case for the conclusion more convincing.

item.title: 4.3 Recognizing a Strengthen the Argument Question
item.description: Lesson Content Most of the time, recognizing a Strengthen the Argument question is easy because these questions utilize predictable language. Which of the following, if true, most strongly supports _____? Which of the following, if true, provides the strongest grounds for _____? Which of the following, if true, most helps to justify _____? Which of the following, if true, provides the best reason for _____? Which of the following, if true, would strengthen _____?

item.title: 4.4 Strategy for Strengthen the Argument Questions
item.description: Lesson Content To find the correct answer to a Strengthen question, follow this 6-step strategy. Step 1 – Carefully read and understand the passage. The foundation of arriving at the correct answer to a Strengthen question is understanding the passage. The importance of understanding the passage cannot be overemphasized. So, your first job in answering a Strengthen question is to carefully read the passage, developing understanding of what the passage says and noting key details of the passage.

item.title: 4.5 Common Ways to Strengthen an Argument
item.description: Lesson Content While there are many ways in which an argument can be strengthened, the following five ways show up consistently in GMAT Strengthen the Argument questions: Provide a Premise to Support or Add Support for the Conclusion Explicitly State an Assumption upon which the Argument Depends Address an Issue that Could Affect the Validity of the Conclusion Rule out an Alternate Cause of an Effect Confirm a Conclusion by Changing, Adding, or Eliminating a Variable Let’s discuss each of these.

item.title: 4.6 Provide a Premise to Support or Add Support for the Conclusion
item.description: Lesson Content In some Strengthen the Argument questions, the passages contain conclusions without any supporting premises. 💡MUST KNOW: In some Strengthen the Argument questions, the passages contain conclusions without any supporting premises. In other questions, the premises within the arguments only partially support the conclusions. In any of these cases, an effective way for an answer choice to strengthen the argument is to provide a premise, which will either provide the only support for the conclusion or provide further support for the conclusion alongside existing premises.

item.title: 4.7 Explicitly State an Assumption Upon Which the Argument Depends
item.description: Lesson Content As you well know, an assumption is a piece of missing information that must be true in order for the logic of the argument to hold. Every argument relies upon many assumptions; it isn’t possible to include every single piece of relevant information in an argument. When an assumption is uncontroversial (it is something that “everyone would agree is true”), the presence in the argument of that unstated assumption has no impact on the strength of the argument.

item.title: 4.8 Address an Issue That Could Affect the Validity of the Conclusion
item.description: Lesson Content Often, in a scenario described in a Strengthen the Argument passage, there is room for issues that could undermine the conclusion. For example, imagine if the CEO of an electric car company made the claim that his electric cars are much more environmentally friendly than their gas-powered counterparts because, while gas-powered cars emit greenhouse gases, electric cars do not. In making that argument, he makes many assumptions, one of which is that there is no part of the electric car production process that could cause enough harm to the environment that his argument would be unsound.

item.title: 4.9 Rule Out an Alternate Cause of an Effect
item.description: Lesson Content In the arguments found in Strengthen the Argument questions, authors will often use cause-and-effect reasoning; in other words, they will link an effect to a cause. 💡MUST KNOW: Whenever an author makes a cause-and-effect claim, he assumes that there cannot be an alternative explanation for the effect observed. In other words, he assumes that his cause-and-effect claim is completely accurate. In other words, whenever we can rule out an alternative explanation for an observed effect, we have strengthened the argument.

item.title: 4.10 Confirming a Conclusion by Changing, Adding, or Eliminating a Variable
item.description: Lesson Content You are probably familiar with how many scientific experiments work: a hypothesis is presented, and then experiments are done in which variables are changed to see how changes in variables affect results. For instance, a researcher who feels that children’s eating sugar makes learning difficult for them could confirm that hypothesis by having a control group and an experimental group. If the children in the control group were to learn more easily than the children in the experimental group, the researcher could say that the results support the hypothesis that sugar consumption was causing the children in the experimental group to have difficulty learning.

item.title: 4.11 The Difference Between Trap Choices and Correct Answers in Strengthen Questions
item.description: Lesson Content In most Strengthen the Argument questions, along with the correct answer, there are one or more answer choices that can appear to strengthen the argument while not actually doing so. Noticing the difference between these so called “trap” choices and the correct answer is generally the key final move in choosing the correct answer to a Strengthen the Argument question. Trap Choice: A trap choice in a Strengthen the Argument question says something that is somehow related to the argument and that seems to strengthen the argument, and at the same time, what the trap choice says is not logically connected to the argument in a way such that it strengthens the argument.

item.title: 4.12 Common Incorrect Choices in Strengthen Questions
item.description: Lesson Content Over time, the types of common incorrect choices that appear in Strengthen questions have been predictable. Let’s begin by discussing incorrect choices that do not affect the strength of arguments in any way.

item.title: 4.13 Strengthen Incorrect Choice Type 1: Provides Information that Does Not Affect the Argument in any Way
item.description: Lesson Content The most blatantly wrong answer choices in Strengthen questions simply do not affect the arguments. In many cases, such choices are fairly easy to eliminate. Consider the following example: The government of Golado has recently increased the taxes imposed on the sale of all alcoholic beverages in Golado. While the tax increase is sure to result in a significant decrease in consumption of alcohol in Golado, government officials believe that increasing the taxes on sales of alcohol will result in a reduction in Golado’s government budget gap.

item.title: 4.14 Strengthen Incorrect Choice Type 2: Information That Seems to Confirm the Validity of a Premise
item.description: Lesson Content In Strengthen questions, there can be choices that seem to strengthen arguments by providing information that seems to confirm the validity of premises. 💡MUST KNOW: Some incorrect answers to Strengthen questions are incorrect because their only function is to seem to confirm the validity of premises. Since the premises are already considered facts, these answer choices do not affect the strength of arguments. Consider the following example: Over the past decade, most people in Ostazia have significantly increased their consumption of fresh fruit.

item.title: 4.15 Strengthen Incorrect Choice Type 3: Choice That Supports the Wrong Conclusion
item.description: Lesson Content As we know, to arrive at the correct answer to a Strengthen question, we have to clearly understand exactly what conclusion we are seeking to support. 💡MUST KNOW: To arrive at the correct answer to a Strengthen question, we have to clearly understand exactly what conclusion we are seeking to support. If we don’t, we might fall for an answer choice that supports a conclusion that is similar to the conclusion we are seeking to support but is not that conclusion.

item.title: 4.16 Strengthen Incorrect Choice Type 4: Explains a Part of the Stimulus
item.description: Lesson Content We must always remember that the premises in Critical Reasoning arguments are considered factual. 💡MUST KNOW: The premises in Critical Reasoning arguments are considered factual. In other words, the premises are facts. Since there is no way to strengthen a fact, there is no way to strengthen a premise. Therefore, it’s important to know that answer choices that explain part of a passage do not strengthen an argument because, as we just said, premises are facts, and even if we were to provide a 100-page essay explaining a fact, we couldn’t strengthen it.

item.title: 4.17 Strengthen Incorrect Choice Type 5: Information not Well Connected to the Scenario in the Argument
item.description: Lesson Content A typical argument in a Strengthen question is based on a scenario that involves, for example, a particular place, company, phenomenon, or point in time. 💡MUST KNOW: In answering a Strengthen question, we must take care not to select as correct choices that aren’t well-connected to the scenario in the argument. So, what does it mean to be “not well-connected?” The truth of the matter is that what the GMAT considers not well-connected might be different from what we’d expect (sorry – don’t kill the messenger).

item.title: 4.18 Strengthen Incorrect Choice Type 6: Pseudo-Strengthener
item.description: Lesson Content Some answers to Strengthen questions are subtly written in a way such that they seem to strengthen the arguments, and, at the same time, they don’t really get the job done. 💡MUST KNOW: Some answers to Strengthen questions are subtly written in a way such that they seem to strengthen the arguments, and, at the same time, they don’t really get the job done. So, we could call them “pseudo-strengtheners.

item.title: 4.19 Strengthen Incorrect Choice Type 7: Weakens the Argument
item.description: Lesson Content If the goal in answering a Strengthen question is to find an answer choice that strengthens the argument, then clearly a choice that weakens the argument must be incorrect. However, when a test-taker is under the pressure of the test, answers that weaken rather than strengthen the arguments in Strengthen questions can be tempting because they are closely related to the given information and, thus, can seem to provide just the type of information needed to answer the question.

item.title: 4.20 Other Tricky Question Stems: “EXCEPT,” “NOT,” “LEAST”
item.description: Lesson Content Strengthen the Argument questions will sometimes include words such as “except,” “not,” or “least” in their question stems to make the questions a bit more challenging. Each of the following, if true, provides additional support for the above conclusion EXCEPT: If all of the following are true, which of them does not provide further justification for the contention made? Assuming that all of the following statements are true, which does the LEAST to strengthen the argument?
